front: Composer: member of the Second Viennese School; student of Schoenberg; best known for the operas Wozzeck and Lulu
Composer: Opus 1 is a one-movement piano sonata in B minor using Schoenberg's 'developing variation' technique
Composer: performance of the Altenberg-Lieder caused a riot known as the Skandalkonzert
Opera: title character, a soldier, drowns himself in a pond after murdering his wife Marie, who had been raped by the Drum Major
Opera: three acts structured respectively as character pieces, a five-movement symphony, and six inventions
Opera: opening structured as a Baroque suite; a fast out-of-tune polka on an onstage piano introduces a bar scene
Opera: the same baritone plays both Dr. Schon and Jack the Ripper, who kills the title character and the lesbian Countess Geschwitz
Opera: each character has their own tone row, all derived from a single row based on a diatonic hexachord; introduced the vibraphone to the orchestra
Opera: a palindromic silent film plays during an interlude depicting the heroine's jailing, cholera, and hospitalization
Opera: left unfinished; prologue has a ringmaster call the title character his 'prized snake'; character Alwa is considered a self-portrait of the composer
Concerto: dedicated 'to the memory of an angel,' referring to Manon Gropius (daughter of Alma Mahler and architect Walter Gropius)
Concerto: finale contains variations on Bach's chorale 'Es ist genug,' whose opening notes match the end of the tone row
Concerto: first movement quotes a Carinthian folk song; soloist enters with a four-note arpeggio on open strings after the harp arpeggiate perfect fifths
Concerto: commissioned by Louis Krasner; tone row begins with ascending thirds (first nine notes), ends with ascending whole steps (last three)
String Quartet: Theodor Adorno called it a 'latent opera'; uses an 'A-B-H-F' motif encoding the composer's love affair with Hanna Fuchs-Robettin
String Quartet: sixth movement ('largo desolato') quotes from Tristan und Isolde; fifth movement marked 'presto delirando'
